---
title: "Methodology"
---
## Water Temperature Data

The data that was used to evaluate water temperature was taken from @WaterTemperature. More specifically, the NOAA Center for Operational Oceanographic Products and Services (CO-OPS) is responsible for harvesting the data. The purpose of harvesting the data is to provide data that helps businesses and civilians in coastal areas stay informed about what is going on in the waters that surround them.

NOAA uses a sensor with an ID "E1" to record the temperature of the water. The sensor is not always stationed in one spot, so some years in recent history had no data for the location that was used. For this report, the range of time went from March 9th, 2022 to March 8th, 2023. This was the most recent time frame where data was available for 365 consecutive days.

NOAA CO-OPS has Oceanographers that make sure the data is accurate and reliable. They also hire field technical experts to maintain the measuring tools and instruments and ensure they operate to meet rigorous scientific standards. The data is then handled by data scientists to clean and organize the data to be used.

## Electrical Demand Data

The data set used to observe energy demand was taken from @energy_demand. On their website, users can access many different graphs that display different information about the electrical usage and production in America. The data that was used in this report came from a customized graph/table that showed the electrical demand of the Southeastern region of the country (this region includes the entire state of Alabama). The same time frame that was used for the water temperature data was also used here.

The EIA's information is gathered by electrical companies and other businesses in the power industry. They report their own data to EIA, where they combine it with companies all over the country and present it in one place.

## Analysis Methodology

The two data sets that were used in this report were combined by using the "mutate" function to turn the columns in each data set that dealt with time, and turned them into a numeric value. From their, an "inner_join" function was used to combine the two data sets by the time stamp column that both data sets now share. After the two data sets were joined together, the water temperature column in the NOAA data set was compared with the energy demand column in the EIA data set in a scatter plot. A regression line and residual plots were added and a summary was generated to see the correlation between the two factors.
